worth_it_explanation: Reporting accuracy decides whether the numbers a publisher acts on match the money that arrives, so it sits underneath every other monetization decision. A revenue figure that drifts from the remittance, or two reports that disagree because their timezones or dimensions are set differently, leads a publisher to optimize toward a number that is not real. The check is a reconciliation and configuration review of reports the publisher already pulls from TripleLift and from their ad server, so it costs reading and matching time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific gap a publisher can close.
intro: Reporting accuracy is about whether the revenue and fill a publisher reads from TripleLift can be trusted as a basis for decisions. Two questions sit at the center of it. First, do the reported numbers reconcile against the payments that actually land, so reported revenue is not drifting from remittance. Second, are the report dimensions and timezones configured so a TripleLift report and an ad server report describe the same impressions over the same window, so the two can be compared at all. These checks confirm that the numbers driving yield decisions are grounded in money received and lined up across systems rather than taken at face value.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against actual payments
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When reported revenue is never matched against the remittance that arrives, a gap between the two can persist unseen. Discrepancies, deductions, or invalid-traffic clawbacks reduce the payment below the report, and a publisher optimizing toward the reported figure is steering on a number that money never backed. The risk grows because the error compounds across every yield decision made on the inflated figure.

educate.base:
A revenue report is a record of what an exchange counted, and a payment is the money that arrives after that count is settled. The two are related but they are not the same number. Between the impression-level revenue a Supply-Side Platform (SSP) reports and the amount it remits, several adjustments can apply. Invalid traffic detected after the fact gets clawed back. Demand-side disputes and discrepancies reduce the settled amount. Revenue-share terms, minimum thresholds, and currency conversion each move the figure. A report read in isolation tells a publisher what was counted, not what was paid.

Reconciliation closes that gap. The practice is to take the revenue a publisher reads from the TripleLift report for a settled period, set it next to the remittance and the payment statement for that same period, and confirm the two agree within a known tolerance. Where they do not, the difference should trace to a stated reason, for example a documented discrepancy adjustment or an invalid-traffic deduction, rather than an unexplained shortfall. A publisher who never runs this comparison cannot tell whether the reported revenue is the revenue they will be paid.

The same logic applies to fill. A reported fill rate that the publisher never checks against impressions that actually monetized can drift from reality, especially where measured and billable impressions are counted differently across systems. Fill that looks healthy in the report but does not convert into paid impressions points to a counting difference worth tracing.

A few signs point to revenue and fill that are not reconciled:
  • No process exists for matching the TripleLift revenue report against the remittance or payment statement for the same settled period
  • The publisher cannot explain the difference between a recent report total and the corresponding payment beyond assuming they match
  • Reported fill is taken at face value with no check against impressions that actually monetized and paid
You may want to confirm that reported revenue and fill are matched against money received on a regular cadence. One approach is to reconcile each settled period against its remittance and trace any difference to a documented reason before acting on the reported figures.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, revenue can swing with seasonality and traffic spikes, so a single month's report read alone can hide a reconciliation gap that a steadier period would expose. You may want to reconcile each settled period against its remittance regardless of how the traffic moved, so a high-revenue month is matched to its payment with the same rigor as a quiet one.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, ad revenue is one line among several, so a gap between reported and paid ad revenue can blend into the larger picture and go unnoticed. You may want to reconcile the TripleLift report against its remittance as a standalone check rather than folding it into a blended revenue view where a shortfall would be hard to isolate.

learn_more.source_title: Analytics for Prebid.js for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed revenue reporting and payment-statement documentation for publishers is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Pull the TripleLift revenue and fill report for a settled period, set it next to the remittance or payment statement for the same period, and confirm the two agree or that any difference traces to a documented reason.

triplelift_reporting.label: TripleLift reporting
steps: Pull the revenue report for a period that has already settled and been paid, broken out by day so the window is unambiguous.
Record the reported revenue total and the reported fill rate for that period.
Locate the remittance or payment statement that covers the same settled period and record the amount paid.

Match the reported revenue against the payment, and trace any difference to a stated reason such as a discrepancy adjustment, an invalid-traffic deduction, or a revenue-share term, rather than leaving it unexplained.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Pull the ad server report for the same period and channel that routes through TripleLift, with impressions and revenue.
Compare the impressions the ad server counted as filled against the impressions TripleLift reported as monetized to see whether the fill figures describe the same events.
Note any case where reported fill looks healthy but the paid or monetized impressions do not support it, which points to a counting difference to trace.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a settled period, the reported revenue, the reported fill, and the amount actually paid, along with whether any difference is explained by a documented reason. A setup where reported revenue is reconciled against the remittance on a regular cadence and differences trace to stated adjustments passes. A setup where no reconciliation exists, or where a difference between report and payment cannot be explained, is a fail recorded with the period and the size of the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the reported revenue, reported fill, and amount paid for a settled period above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} reads revenue and fill from TripleLift without reconciling them against the payments that actually arrive. A reported figure records what the exchange counted, not what was paid, and invalid-traffic clawbacks, discrepancy adjustments, and revenue-share terms can move the remittance below the report, so optimizing toward an unreconciled number means steering on revenue that money never backed. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling each settled period against its remittance on a regular cadence, tracing any difference to a documented reason such as a discrepancy adjustment or an invalid-traffic deduction before acting on the reported figures. After the first reconciliation, set a recurring check so reported revenue is matched to money received rather than taken at face value.

audit_checks.title: Report dimensions and timezones are not aligned across systems
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a TripleLift report and an ad server report use different timezones or different dimension definitions, the two describe different sets of impressions and cannot be compared directly. A publisher reading them side by side sees a discrepancy that is an artifact of configuration rather than a real revenue or fill difference, which sends investigation toward a problem that does not exist and leaves real gaps harder to find.

educate.base:
Two reports can only be compared when they describe the same events over the same window. That sounds obvious, yet the default settings across systems frequently break it. A TripleLift report and an ad server report can each carry their own timezone, so a report set to Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) and a report set to a local timezone split the same day at different hours. Read side by side, they appear to disagree on revenue and impressions for any given date, when the only difference is where each one drew the day boundary.

Dimensions create the same problem in a different place. The grain a report is broken out by, for example by ad unit, by placement, by site, or by format, has to mean the same thing in both systems for a comparison to hold. A native placement counted as one ad unit in TripleLift but rolled into a broader unit in the ad server will not line up, and the mismatch reads as a discrepancy even though both numbers are correct for their own definition. Comparing a report at one grain against a report at another grain compares two different things.

The metric definitions matter too. Measured impressions, billable impressions, and rendered impressions are not interchangeable, and a fill or revenue comparison built on one definition in one system and a different definition in the other will not reconcile. Aligning the comparison means choosing a timezone, a dimension grain, and an impression definition that both systems can produce, then holding the reports to that shared basis.

A few signs point to dimensions and timezones that are not aligned:
  • The TripleLift report and the ad server report run on different timezones, so the same calendar date covers different hours in each
  • Reports are compared at different dimension grains, e.g., a per-placement breakout against a per-site total, with no common level
  • A persistent daily discrepancy between the two systems shrinks or disappears once the timezones and dimensions are matched, which marks it as a configuration artifact
You may want to confirm that any cross-system comparison uses a shared timezone, a common dimension grain, and a consistent impression definition. One approach is to set both reports to the same timezone and the same breakout before reading them against each other.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site with many sections and placement types, the dimension grain is easy to mismatch because TripleLift and the ad server may group placements differently. You may want to agree on a common breakout, for example by placement, that both systems can produce, so a section-level comparison is not read against a site-level total.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, reporting is often pulled into a finance or analytics view that runs on the business's local timezone, which can differ from the timezone the ad reports use. You may want to confirm the ad reports and the business reporting share a timezone before comparing them, so a revenue figure is not split across the wrong day.

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's report-builder documentation covering its timezone and dimension settings for publishers is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the timezone, dimension grain, and impression definition each report uses, then confirm that any cross-system comparison holds both reports to the same shared basis.

triplelift_reporting.label: TripleLift reporting
steps: Open the report settings and record the timezone the TripleLift report uses, e.g., UTC or a named local timezone.
Record the dimensions the report is broken out by, such as ad unit, placement, site, or format, and how each is defined.
Note which impression metric the report counts, e.g., measured, billable, or rendered, so the basis is explicit.

ad_server_reporting.label: Ad server reporting
steps: Open the ad server report settings and record its timezone, its dimension grain, and its impression definition.
Compare those settings against the TripleLift report and note every place the timezone, dimension, or impression definition differs.
Re-run both reports on a shared timezone and a common dimension grain, then check whether a previous daily discrepancy shrinks, which would mark it as a configuration artifact rather than a real gap.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the timezone, the dimension grain, and the impression definition for the TripleLift report and for the ad server report, alongside whether cross-system comparisons hold both to a shared basis. A setup where the two reports are compared on a common timezone, a common dimension grain, and a consistent impression definition passes. A setup where the reports run on different timezones or dimension grains and are compared anyway is a fail recorded with the settings that diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the timezone, dimension grain, and impression definition for each report above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} compares TripleLift reports against ad server reports without aligning their timezones, dimension grains, and impression definitions. When two reports draw the day boundary at different hours or break inventory out at different levels, they describe different sets of impressions, so a discrepancy between them can be a configuration artifact rather than a real revenue or fill gap. {{voice_recommend}} setting both reports to a shared timezone, a common dimension grain, and a consistent impression definition before reading them against each other, then rechecking whether a standing discrepancy shrinks once the basis matches. After alignment, hold every cross-system comparison to that shared basis so the numbers describe the same events.
